original_abstract:
The efficient capture and immobilization of radiological iodine ($^{129}$I) is a critical priority for ensuring the safety of nuclear energy production and long-term waste management. Silver-exchanged mordenite (Ag-MOR) has long served as the benchmark adsorbent for this application. However, in the complex off-gas environments typical of nuclear fuel reprocessing, achieving high selectivity for diverse iodine-bearing species remains an engineering challenge. A fundamental, molecular-level understanding of how silver-functionalized zeolites interact with organic iodine compounds is currently lacking. 

In this study, we investigate the structural evolution and spatial distribution of methyl iodide ($\text{CH}_3\text{I}$) captured within Ag-MOR using a comprehensive suite of analytical techniques, including infrared spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis coupled with mass spectrometry (TGA-MS), micro-X-ray fluorescence ($\mu$-XRF), powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), and pair distribution function (PDF) analysis. Our results demonstrate that iodine is sequestered within the zeolite channels through the formation of AgI nanoparticles, whose dimensions are constrained by the mordenite pore architecture. This confirms that the sorbate is stabilized through both physical confinement and chemical conversion. Simultaneously, the zeolite framework acts as a surface catalyst for the organic fragment, facilitating the formation of surface methoxy species (SMS) which subsequently react to produce dimethyl ether and methanol as downstream byproducts. These findings provide essential insights into the dual-functional role of silver-zeolites in decontaminating complex nuclear off-gas streams.
humanized_abstract:
Radiological iodine ($^{129}$I) removal and containment is a pressing need for the nuclear energy industry. Silver-exchanged mordenite (Ag-MOR) has been the industry standard material for containing radiological iodine. However, in the complicated off-gas environment associated with nuclear fuel reprocessing, selective removal of a wide range of different iodine species has proven to be an outstanding materials challenge. There is no current understanding at the molecular level of the interactions between silver-functionalized zeolites and organic iodine species.

The structural evolution and spatial distribution of the Point Source Contaminant Methyl Iodide (CH3I) species captured by an Ag-exchanged mordenite zeolite material known as Ag-MOR has been probed via a multimodal approach involving in-situ infrared spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis with on-line mass spectrometry (TGA-MS), micro-X-ray fluorescence (μ-XRF), powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and pair distribution function (PDF) analysis. Our experimental data indicated that iodine species released from CH3I are actually captured within the zeolite pore structure in the form of AgI nanoparticles. The average size of the nanoparticles is within the channel sizes of mordenite zeolites, indicating that the iodine species are trapped physically and chemically in the zeolite structure. The zeolite structure is also believed to act as a catalytic surface for the organic part of the CH3I molecule where surface methoxy species (SMS) are generated and subsequently react to yield dimethyl ether and methanol as secondary products. This study on the reaction of CH3I species with Ag-MOR provides significant contributes to understanding the functionalities of catalytic material for removal of Point Source Contaminants (PSCs) from nuclear off-gas.
